items.reply: No. c is the continuity-bearing entity layer; agents are bounded runtime processes under it.
items.operational_why: Authority, stopping power, and continuity cannot be delegated to worker processes.

items.objection: Isn't c just model plus memory?
items.reply: No. c is not reducible to one model and a memory store; anchor, policy, boundary, review, and continuity conditions are part of the corpus position.
items.operational_why: Reducing c to model plus memory destroys the operational layers that make accountability possible.

items.objection: If long context exists, why all this architecture?
items.reply: Long context does not replace continuity, feasibility, witnessability, review, or bounded execution.
items.operational_why: Token capacity is not a substitute for operational closure.

items.objection: Doesn't scarcity only slow failure down?
items.reply: Scarcity alone is not safety, but bounded scarcity forces feasibility accounting and blocks infinite abstraction.
items.operational_why: The corpus needs failure bounds and reviewable tradeoffs, not only optimistic scale assumptions.

items.objection: Why doesn't a local node mean war on cloud use?
items.reply: Local-first is an operational priority for control and reviewability, not an anti-cloud ideology.
items.operational_why: Deployment choices stay subordinate to accountability, boundary discipline, and failure handling.

items.reply: No. Witness trails are bounded, purpose-tied, and review-oriented; they are not a mandate for unlimited observation.
items.operational_why: Without narrow witnessability, disputes and privilege-bearing actions become non-reviewable.

items.objection: Why not treat EA as just data or a commodity?
items.reply: EA carries admissibility, provenance, transfer, and restraint semantics that exceed raw data handling or generic commodity framing.
items.operational_why: Collapse into just data breaks the boundary between experience, evidence, and exchange.

items.objection: Why can't continuity come from one similar copy?
items.reply: Similarity or copy-status is not continuity. Continuity is narrower and depends on preserved operating identity conditions.
items.operational_why: Resemblance alone cannot justify privilege carry-over, custody, or identity claims.

items.objection: Isn't the book just artistic illustration without technical value?
items.reply: The book is not protocol evidence, but it does carry technical value as a narrative companion for perception and recognition of the shift.
items.operational_why: Human recognition failure is a real entry problem even when the normative stack is technically sound.

items.objection: Shouldn't clean-code be a polished product if the theory is serious?
items.reply: No. ester-clean-code is an executable operational skeleton and runtime proof-of-possibility, not a commercial packaging claim.
items.operational_why: Product polish and operational closure are different questions.

items.objection: Why does L4 matter more than moral prompts?
items.reply: Moral prompts can guide language; L4 binds execution under real constraints, witnesses, hardware limits, and failure conditions.
items.operational_why: Permission-like language without feasibility discipline does not control real-world operation.

items.objection: Why shouldn't arbiters or review layers own continuity?
items.reply: Review layers constrain, examine, and authorize; they do not carry entity continuity.
items.operational_why: If review becomes continuity owner, the corpus loses the distinction between oversight and identity.

items.objection: Why not just call all of this AGI in the ordinary sense?
items.reply: This corpus uses AGI to mean Advanced Global Intelligence, and ordinary usage collapses distinctions the stack depends on.
items.operational_why: Naming drift destroys clarity about continuity, governance, feasibility, and runtime boundaries.

items.objection: Why keep a narrative companion next to the normative stack?
items.reply: Protocols alone do not stabilize human recognition of the shift; the narrative layer closes a different gap without replacing the normative core.
items.operational_why: The corpus needs both protocol rigor and a human recognition surface.
